intro: The Book of Proverbs is a collection of moral and philosophical wisdom literature comprising eight distinct sections attributed to Solomon (chs. 1–9, 10–22:16, 25–29), the "men of Hezekiah" (chs. 25–29), Agur (ch. 30), and Lemuel's mother (ch. 31). The book's foundational declaration is "The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" (Prov. 1:7; 9:10), establishing that true practical wisdom is inseparable from right relationship with God. Chapters 1–9 contain extended poems on the two ways — Wisdom and Folly — personifying wisdom as a woman who built her house and calls men to her feast (Prov. 8–9). Chapter 8 presents Lady Wisdom present at creation, which the New Testament reads in relation to Christ (Col. 1:15–17; John 1:1–3). The book closes with the poem of the virtuous wife (Prov. 31:10–31), an acrostic presenting embodied wisdom in domestic life.
